did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper used a hybrid Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) and Autoregressive Integrated Moving Average (ARIMA) model (CNN-ARIMA) for skin cancer classification. The CNN served as an automatic feature extractor from dermoscopic images, and the ARIMA model was integrated into the fully connected layer to evaluate similarity and classify three types of skin cancer (melanoma, basal cell carcinoma, and squamous cell carcinoma) using the ISIC dataset.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper presents original experimental work proposing a hybrid CNN-ARIMA model for skin cancer classification. It details the methodology using the ISIC dataset, describes the experimental setup (training/validation splits, hyperparameters), and reports new quantitative results (96.00% accuracy, 0.995 AUC) comparing the proposed method against existing approaches. Although it includes a brief related work section, its primary purpose is to report new technical results rather than summarize prior literature.

title: Skin Cancer Classification using Convolutional Neural Network with Autoregressive Integrated Moving Average
journal: 2021 4th International Conference on Robot Systems and Applications (ICRSA 2021)
brief_summary: This study proposes a hybrid Convolutional Neural Network and Autoregressive Integrated Moving Average (CNN-ARIMA) model to classify three types of skin cancer: melanoma, basal cell carcinoma, and squamous cell carcinoma. The researchers utilized dermoscopic images from the International Skin Imaging Collaboration (ISIC) dataset available on Kaggle, selecting 995 images which were augmented to 4000. The CNN served as an automatic feature extractor, while the ARIMA model was integrated into the fully connected layer to evaluate similarity and perform classification. The model was trained using TensorFlow and Keras with a learning rate of 0.001, achieving a test accuracy of 96.00%, average sensitivity of 96.02%, average specificity of 97.98%, and an AUC of 0.995. The results indicate that the proposed CNN-ARIMA architecture outperforms existing state-of-the-art methods in skin cancer classification tasks.

lesion_types_studied.details: One of the three classes used in the classification experiment. 263 images were categorized accurately in the confusion matrix. Reported sensitivity of 94.95%, specificity of 97.32%, and precision of 94.95%.

One of the three classes used in the classification experiment. 259 images were categorized accurately in the confusion matrix. Reported sensitivity of 96.64%, specificity of 96.80%, and precision of 93.84%.

One of the three classes used in the classification experiment. 246 images were categorized accurately in the confusion matrix. Reported sensitivity of 96.47%, specificity of 99.82%, and precision of 99.60%.

all_task_types.target: Three types of skin cancer: melanoma, basal cell carcinoma, and squamous cell carcinoma
all_task_types.details: Multiclass classification of dermoscopic images into three discrete diagnostic categories (melanoma, basal cell carcinoma, squamous cell carcinoma) using a hybrid CNN-ARIMA model. The model achieved 96.00% test accuracy, 0.995 AUC, and reported per-class sensitivity, specificity, and precision.

datasets_used.name: ISIC Dataset (via Kaggle)
datasets_used.description: Dermoscopic images from the International Skin Imaging Collaboration (ISIC) available on Kaggle. The study selected a subset of 995 RGB dermoscopic images from a larger collection of 2357 images categorized into 9 skin cancer diseases. The images were augmented to 4000 images using intensity adjustment techniques (blur, light, dark) and resized to 32x32 pixels. Hair removal was applied using OpenCV.

use.training: Used for training the CNN-ARIMA model (80% of the augmented dataset, 3200 images).
use.validation: Used for validation (20% of the augmented dataset, 640 images).
use.testing: Used for testing to evaluate performance metrics (20% of the augmented dataset, 800 images).

statistical_methods_used.purpose: To compute per-class and average sensitivity (TPR), specificity (TNR), and precision (PPV) for evaluating classification performance.
To evaluate the model's performance across all classification thresholds and calculate the Area Under the Curve (AUC).

statistical_methods_used.method: Data Augmentation (Intensity Adjustment, Random Shifts, Zooming, Stretching, Rotation)
statistical_methods_used.purpose: To balance the dataset and prevent overfitting by increasing the number of training samples from 995 to 4000.

statistical_methods_used.method: Hyperparameter Search (Grid Search over Learning Rates and ARIMA n values)
statistical_methods_used.purpose: To determine the optimal learning rate (0.001) and ARIMA out-shape parameter (n=9) that maximize test accuracy.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report any formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or significance tests. Performance was evaluated using descriptive metrics (accuracy, sensitivity, specificity, precision, AUC) and compared against existing methods in a table without statistical significance testing.

experiment.name: CNN-ARIMA Model Performance Analysis

hyperparameter_sweep_results.description: Test accuracy (%) for different learning rates and ARIMA n values

experiment.notes: The proposed CNN-ARIMA model outperformed existing state-of-the-art methods in terms of test accuracy, average sensitivity, average specificity, average precision, and AUC. Learning rates of 0.1 and 0.01 resulted in low test accuracy, while 0.001 and 0.0001 provided great performance. The highest test accuracy of 96.00% was achieved with a learning rate of 0.001 and n=9.

architecture_used.main_model: CNN-ARIMA (Hybrid Convolutional Neural Network and Autoregressive Integrated Moving Average)
architecture_used.pretraining: None (The paper explicitly states it did not require transfer learning or fine-tuning techniques)

architecture_details.backbone: Custom CNN with three stages of convolutional layers followed by max-pooling and ReLU activation functions
architecture_details.regularization: Dropout of 0.5 in the CNN architecture
architecture_details.hybrid_component: ARIMA model placed between the flatten layer and the dense layer in the fully connected layer to evaluate similarity

arima_parameters.p: Not specified (output parameter remains same regardless of value)
arima_parameters.d: Not specified (output parameter remains same regardless of value)
arima_parameters.q: Not specified (output parameter remains same regardless of value)

classification_or_output_head.type: Modified fully connected layer with ARIMA model

machine_learning_or_deep_learning_models: CNN-ARIMA (Hybrid Convolutional Neural Network and Autoregressive Integrated Moving Average)
SVM (Support Vector Machine) (used by Filali et al. [10] and Yu et al. [9])
GoogleNet (modified, used by Kassem et al. [13])
Deep Neural Network (DNN) (referenced in related work by Suhermi et al. [20])
LSTM (Long Short-Term Memory) (referenced in related work by Ji et al. [21])
preprocessing_techniques: Hair removal using Open-Source Computer Vision Library (OpenCV) to eliminate fine hair from dermoscopic images
Data augmentation via intensity adjustment techniques (blur, light, dark) to increase dataset size from 995 to 4000 images
Class balancing through augmentation to create a balanced dataset (1333 images for basal cell carcinoma, 1334 for melanoma, 1333 for squamous cell carcinoma)
Image rotation with random angles in the range [-10, 10]

data_augmentation_techniques.details: Applied to original images to generate blur, light, and dark variations, increasing the dataset size from 995 to 4000 images.
Applied using random angles in the range [-10, 10] degrees.
Augmented to create a balanced dataset comprising 1333 images for basal cell carcinoma, 1334 for melanoma, and 1333 for squamous cell carcinoma.

conclusions: The proposed hybrid CNN-ARIMA model successfully classifies three types of skin cancer (melanoma, basal cell carcinoma, and squamous cell carcinoma) with high performance, achieving a test accuracy of 96.00%, average sensitivity of 96.02%, average specificity of 97.98%, average precision of 96.13%, and an AUC of 0.995.

The proposed CNN-ARIMA architecture outperforms existing state-of-the-art methods, including those by Filali et al., Yu et al., Menegola et al., and Kassem et al., as well as pretrained deep neural network architectures enhanced with transfer learning and fine-tuning.

The model does not require prior segmentation, transfer learning, or fine-tuning techniques, relying instead on the CNN for automatic feature extraction and the ARIMA model for similarity evaluation in the fully connected layer.
A learning rate of 0.001 and an ARIMA out-shape parameter (n) of 9 were identified as the optimal hyperparameters for maximizing test accuracy.
High learning rates (0.1 and 0.01) resulted in unstable training processes and low test accuracy, whereas learning rates of 0.001 and 0.0001 provided great performance.
The study demonstrates that combining CNN for feature extraction with ARIMA for classification is an effective approach for skin cancer diagnosis, potentially aiding in early detection technologies.
important_remarks: The study uses a very small subset of the ISIC dataset (995 images from 2357) and artificially balances classes via aggressive data augmentation (increasing to 4000 images), which may not reflect real-world class distributions.
Images were resized to a low resolution of 32x32 pixels, which may discard fine dermoscopic details critical for accurate diagnosis.

The ARIMA model integration is methodologically questionable for image classification; the paper notes that parameters p, d, and q do not influence performance, suggesting the ARIMA component may be functionally redundant or incorrectly implemented.
No external validation was performed; the model was only evaluated on a single internal split of the ISIC dataset, limiting generalizability.
The paper does not report statistical significance tests (e.g., p-values) for the comparison with baseline methods, making it unclear if the performance gains are statistically significant.

The dataset source is a Kaggle upload ('nodoubttome/skin-cancer9-classesisic') rather than the official ISIC challenge datasets, raising concerns about data provenance and potential contamination or non-standard preprocessing.
The study claims to classify three specific skin cancers but does not address the challenge of distinguishing them from benign lesions or other malignant types not included in the study.
The model does not utilize transfer learning or fine-tuning, which is unusual for deep learning in medical imaging and may limit its ability to learn robust features from such a small dataset.
